text: I am working on a flow based programming editor, although its largely js/dom/canvas based, I am currently developing a wasm-compiler so that I can run mini programs in a node using webassembly. These mini programs are compiled at runtime to webassembly using javascript and cached. Also I've got two ways of running a flow, one of them runs in webassembly (the sourcecode is written in rust). So currently this is a hybrid solution but I am planning to investigate the impact of switching to an almost 100% wasm/rust/webgl solution, although I expect that at least for now you will need javascript for some glue code if you want to run in the browser. What are your own plans with wasm?

root.text: Is there anyone else who is interested in building high performance (no DOM, no GC) applications that compile to wasm+native? The only project I’ve found so far that seems really serious about this is Makepad, and they have some ways to go..

text:
>  although I expect that at least for now you will need javascript for some glue code if you want to run in the browser.
Yeah some glue code is currently inevitable but in e.g. makepad.dev it’s extremely limited.
> What are your own plans with wasm?
I’ve been working on high performance web applications for a long time now (<http://remix.com|remix.com>, <http://webviz.io|webviz.io>) and JS is just too slow, so I never want to program in it again. :slightly_smiling_face:

text: I’m not sure I understand the distinction between GC’ed and non GC’ed languages here in terms of performance. My take is that stuff that parallelizes well and offloads most stuff to the GPU will do well regardless of GC.

elements.text: I’m not sure I understand the distinction between GC’ed and non GC’ed languages here in terms of performance. My take is that stuff that parallelizes well and offloads most stuff to the GPU will do well regardless of GC.

text: I largely agree with you, but shutting down the discussion by talking about this as a “religious tenet” is unfortunate. I won’t be engaging further. :wave::skin-tone-2:

elements.text: I largely agree with you, but shutting down the discussion by talking about this as a “religious tenet” is unfortunate. I won’t be engaging further.

text:
No DOM is a really, really different thing than no GC.  What are you really looking for <@UCUNN529Y>?  UI access without a lot of indirection and ceremony?

If you're worried about GC for a core algorithm, figure out how much space you need and allocate upfront.  No need to adopt wholesale Rust, Swift, or Jai — if it was widely available.

As far as I can tell, C++ is the standby for games primarily for unfortunate cultural, historical reasons.  And it's not like C++ is remotely one thing within the games industry: most use some narrow subset.

As far as I can tell, C++ is the standby for games primarily for unfortunate cultural, historical reasons.  And it's not like C++ is remotely one thing within the games industry: most use some narrow subset.

text: I had no intention of shutting down the conversation, <@UCUNN529Y>. Sorry to have offended you with a cheeky comment. What I was gesturing toward is that Jon Blow's videos about the lost art of performance programming have led _many_ to repeat his personal preferences as received wisdom, even though the reality is not nearly so black and white as those talks would have us believe.

elements.text: I had no intention of shutting down the conversation,
. Sorry to have offended you with a cheeky comment. What I was gesturing toward is that Jon Blow's videos about the lost art of performance programming have led
many
to repeat his personal preferences as received wisdom, even though the reality is not nearly so black and white as those talks would have us believe.

text:
In Blow's defense, when pressed, he will usually articulate a more nuanced position.  There's always a tradeoff of speaking one's truth clearly and concisely against including all caveats and side-conditions.

Probably the most important aspect of his perspective is that his team is small and so tool simplicity/tractability have high value.  Friends running significantly bigger teams have enough people power to tackle technical hangups when communication, coordination, and people problems dominate.

Of course a large firm can handle "compliance" — be it to a government, platform, or compiler — in a way that would crush an individual.  I digress.  :scream:

text: I share many of Blow's concerns and understand the value of a strong polemic. At the same time, those who receive his transmissions uncritically often fall a little _too_ hard for the ideas. Of course, the same thing has happened many times over the years -- people who've never used Lisp quoting PG's opinions, recycled Jobs-isms from messianic founders, &c.

text: I just find it strange that blanket statements can be made regarding performance especially for scenarios where there is a ton of rasterization going on.  Raph Levien describes the situation pretty elegantly here - <https://www.janestreet.com/tech-talks/a-taste-of-gpu-compute/>

attachments.title: A Taste of GPU Compute :: Jane Street
attachments.text: Jane Street is a quantitative trading firm and liquidity provider with a unique focus on technology and collaborative problem solving.

text:
Yeah, agreed on all of that, and thanks <@U013ZLJARC7>! The blanket statements also concern me. I’m personally just interested in applications that really push the boundaries of performance, e.g. the last project I worked on was <http://webviz.io|webviz.io>.

There we ran into the limitations of a language like JS — we ended up doing a lot of manual memory management to avoid the overhead of a garbage collector, of non-zero-cost object instantiation and function calls, of DOM elements being fairly expensive when you get into the 10,000-100,000 range, and so on. It was very clear that what we were trying to do did not fit the language well — we ended up with tons of ArrayBuffers and manual operations on them, which was a lot faster, but pretty inconvenient.

So I’m interested in GUI systems in languages where you don’t have all these constraints, and where things like GC/refcount/dynamic dispatch/etc are opt-in, but where the default is much more low-level. This kind of programming is certainly not for every use case, but it seems strange to me that there really isn’t much out there to cater to these more extreme use cases, because surely it can’t be _that_ rare…?

text: Also, in an application like that, it’s not like you can isolate just one part of your application that is the most performance critical — I wish that were the case! The core data that we were operating on easily ran into the many gigabytes, and since pretty much everything builds on top of that and needs to iterate over many objects, there are no easy abstractions. This is probably a pretty extreme use case though. :slightly_smiling_face:

text: Sounds like you were doing the right things — so far as they go.  Everyone let me know if there's a robust, reusable way to scale these sorts of things up.  :pray:  Once you're into the million, billions, iterating isn't such a great idea.  On the UI side, sigh, it's like you know you have a supercomputer under your hands with shaders as a lava pit between you and awesomeness.  Ultimately,  cheating is only trick I know: how do I get most of the benefit of visualizing this data without actually doing it.  It all ends up being bespoke though.

text: <@UCUNN529Y> I also wound up (poorly) writing my own memory manager for js, so I could just stick everything in a single heap for realtime audio work. Here’s a 3D library I recently came across that you might find interesting, a mix of ECS and a single “blittable” array buffer: <https://github.com/actnwit/RhodoniteTS>

attachments.title: actnwit/RhodoniteTS
attachments.text: Rhodonite Web3D Library in TypeScript. Contribute to actnwit/RhodoniteTS development by creating an account on GitHub.

elements.text: I also wound up (poorly) writing my own memory manager for js, so I could just stick everything in a single heap for realtime audio work. Here’s a 3D library I recently came across that you might find interesting, a mix of ECS and a single “blittable” array buffer:

text: Yeah that’s very similar to what we ended up coding manually. The issue is that when you do your own memory management.. you have to do your own memory management. So you get back all the typical issues like memory leaks and pointer issues and such. So it actually makes sense writing an application like that in Rust instead of JS, so you actually get the borrow checker and such. It’s just a better fit.
